summary.title: Car Buying Pattern Change
summary.description: The car buying pattern has changed over the years, with the mean selling price increasing from 0.6 to 11.26 between 1992 and 2021. The count of cars sold per year has also increased, with a significant rise from 1 in 1992 to 232 in 2008. The correlation between 'year' and 'selling_price' is 0.3906673599813431, indicating a positive relationship.

insights.title: Increasing Selling Price
insights.description: The mean selling price has increased from 0.6 to 11.26 between 1992 and 2021, with a correlation of 0.3906673599813431 between 'year' and 'selling_price'. This indicates a positive relationship, with higher prices in later years. For example, the mean selling price in 2010 was 3.684830203106332, while in 2020 it was 9.0804849137931.

insights.title: Skewed Distribution of km_driven
insights.description: The skewness of 'km_driven' is 23.527991420566035, indicating a highly skewed distribution. This suggests that most cars have lower km_driven values, while a few have much higher values. For instance, the correlation between 'year' and 'km_driven' is -0.31028750446647907, indicating a negative relationship.

insights.title: Contradiction in Fuel Type Preferences
insights.description: The mean selling price of diesel cars is 8.234763687391169, while that of petrol cars is 5.028134373771848. However, the correlation between 'year' and 'selling_price' is positive, which might contradict the expectation that diesel cars would have higher prices due to their higher mean selling price. Further investigation is needed to resolve this contradiction.

insights.title: Increasing Sales Volume
insights.description: The count of cars sold per year has increased significantly, from 1 in 1992 to 232 in 2008. This trend is expected to continue, with the count of cars sold per year and fuel type also showing an increasing pattern. For example, the count of cars sold per year and fuel type in 2010 was 47, while in 2020 it was 1274.
